MessageNote: Before I had the seeds implanted I was asked if I had a weak urine flow, problems emptying my bladder, and blood in my stool. I didn't have any of these symptoms. I have now been having weak urine flow and it takes longer to empty my bladder plus I see blood in my stool. I called my primary care provider and they instructed me to go to the emergency room and I am presently at [Name] hospital in [state A]. They were concerned that I may have a bladder infection causing the weak urine flow. They were also concerned about the consistent blood in my stool because I take a blood thinner. Do you have any recommendations?
Response.1:
Dear [Name],
I understand you are experiencing weak urine flow and difficulty emptying your bladder. This can sometimes occur after radiation therapy due to inflammation or other factors. It is good that you are at the hospital for evaluation. I recommend a urinalysis and culture to check for any infections. If an infection is ruled out, medications like alpha-blockers (e.g., tamsulosin) can help improve urine flow. Please follow the advice of the medical team at [Name] Hospital and keep us updated on your condition.
Best regards,
Dr.[Name], M.D.

Hello [Name], I am sorry to hear you are experiencing bothersome symptoms. Unfortunately what you are experiencing is not uncommon post radiation therapy. Typically urinary function improves within 2-3 weeks post radiation therapy. If you are experiencing difficulty emptying your bladder, weak stream, or urinary frequency it may be beneficial for you to start Flomax if it is real bothersome to you. Flomax is a medication that is to be taken once daily in the evening. It works by relaxing your pelvic muscles allowing you empty your bladder easier. If this is something you are interested please let us know and we can send a prescription to your preferred pharmacy. We spoke with Dr. [Name] to let him know you have been experiencing blood in the stools. He believes this is radiation induced proctitis (bowel inflammation). We would recommend continued monitoring for now as symptoms should improve over the next couple weeks. Obviously at any point if you become lightheaded or blood significant increases please present to the emergency department for further assessment. Please let us know if you have any other questions or concerns. Thanks, [Name], RNRadiation Oncology

MessageNote: Hello [Name],One more thing that I did want to ask and forgot with the last message. The last 6-8 weeks I have been feeling tired and somewhat a lack of ambition. Is that a possible side effect of the radiation? If so will this eventually subside? Just thought I should ask.thank  you,[Name]
Response.1:
Dear [Name],

Thank you for reaching out. Fatigue can indeed be a delayed side effect of radiation therapy, but it is important to rule out other potential causes. I recommend the following steps:

Blood Tests: To check for anemia, thyroid function, and other potential issues.
Lifestyle: Ensure you are getting adequate rest, nutrition, and hydration.
Follow-Up: If the fatigue persists, we may need to explore other causes such as depression or other medical conditions.
Please let us know if you have any other symptoms or if you would like to schedule a follow-up appointment to discuss this further.

Best regards, Dr. [Name]

Mr. [Name],Your recent radiation treatment could cause some fatigue, but it should be subsided by this point.  Make sure you are eating healthy, drinking enough water, and exercising.  If you are doing all these things and still fatigued, you may wish to check in with your primary care provider for an evaluation.  Thanks, [Name]

MessageNote: QUESTIONS:1.  Is Flomax prn or do I take it regularly for some undefined length of time?  For the record, I have taken only two caps and have not experienced any dizziness during the two or three times I've gotten out of bed at night.2.  I've been experiencing an unusual amount of borborygmus and flatulence this past week.  Initially it was the Brussell sprouts I had early in the wk, but the gas continued after they were "gone."   Maybe the answer is in all the reading I've received, but it is easier to ask you Folks.  Is this from the enemas, the treatment, the Flomax, or what?  In normal times I'd wait this out for a couple more days, but these aren't normal times, so here I am.Parting Statement:  That is an incredible operation there.  The logistics, the coordination, the staff, all of it was incredible.  Simply amazing.  Between the general functioning and the equipment, my mind was constantly and officially boggled.With appreciation and thanks to ALL,[Name]
Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for your questions.

Flomax Usage: Flomax (tamsulosin) is typically taken once daily, usually in the evening. It is important to take it regularly to maintain its effectiveness. If you experience any side effects such as dizziness, please let us know, and we can adjust your treatment plan accordingly.

Gastrointestinal Symptoms: The borborygmus and flatulence you are experiencing could be related to several factors, including dietary changes, the use of enemas, or the radiation treatment itself. It is less likely to be caused by Flomax. If these symptoms persist or worsen, please let us know, and we can explore further options to manage them.

We appreciate your kind words about our operation and staff. We strive to provide the best care possible.

Best regards, Dr. [Name]

Mr. [Name],Thank you for your questions and kind words.Please take the Flomax daily (not PRN) for the next 3 weeks. You may then try coming off of it, as the urinary symptoms from radiation typically start to subside around this time. However, if your symptoms persist, this is not atypical, and you can restart the Flomax in that case. Flomax can be taken long term without concerns.The gassiness could be from the radiation. It would not be from the enemas or Flomax. If it is from the radiation, we would expect that this would improve over the next several weeks also. In the meantime, you may try modifying your diet and using Gas-X (simethicone), which as an over-the-counter medication.Dr. [Name]-

MessageNote: Good Day, Doctors.After I started taking Flomax, urination and control were no problem. In fact, as weeks passed, I became super-pleased with my bladder control and the urges were less frequent.  I wasn't getting those semi-false, tingling signals to urinate and then have very little to pee. Also, since my occasional leakage issue ceased, I quit using the occasional pad. I couldn't believe that, but I was happy that I could trust my control again.Until last week. First there were a couple incidents where I dribbled minutely as I "readied" to pee. That bothered me, but I thought maybe I just didn't focus enough on "holding it," so to speak. But this past week I have been getting those tingling urges to pee sooner than I had before and I seem to again be having difficulty clearing my tubes. By that I mean that I have dribbled some drops-worth in my drawers a couple times. That is why I used the occasional pad in the first place and now it is feeling like the Bad Old Days are back.My first impulse was to schedule a PSA test here, but then I thought I should have faith in the plan. But then I thought that maybe something IS wrong and catching it sooner would be better than later. Then I wondered if it was my coffee intake or if my increased fat load (my pinch-an-inch is now grab-the-flab) could be affecting my bladder function and that is when I decided to reach out to you.I think it is just a head-game, but I thought I would still ask for your thoughts [Name]
Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your concerns. It is not uncommon for patients to experience changes in urinary symptoms following prostate cancer treatment. The symptoms you described, such as dribbling and increased frequency of urges, could be related to several factors, including the effects of radiation therapy and the use of Flomax.

Here are a few recommendations:

Continue Flomax: Please continue taking Flomax as prescribed. If you have stopped taking it, you may restart it to see if it helps alleviate your symptoms.
Monitor Symptoms: Keep a record of your urinary symptoms, including frequency, urgency, and any incidents of dribbling. This will help us better understand the pattern and severity of your symptoms.
Diet and Hydration: Consider reducing your intake of caffeine and other bladder irritants. Ensure you are staying well-hydrated but avoid excessive fluid intake before bedtime.
Follow-Up Appointment: If your symptoms persist or worsen, we may need to schedule a follow-up appointment to evaluate your condition further.
Please let us know if you have any other questions or if you would like to schedule an appointment.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

Mr. [Name] Thank you for the update your situation.  To answer your 1st question, you can assume those appointments in May are accurate and plan to travel accordingly.  I am sorry about any confusion about a missed appointment, it sounds like this was something that perhaps was done outside of our department and was simply an error.As for your urinary symptoms, we can see some delayed flares after radiation that happen 6-18 months after treatment.  These typically manifest as a variety of different symptoms, but what you describe would fit in that realm.  This is often a transient symptom profile that resolves with time.  It is often caused by a delayed inflammatory response in the prostate.  Certainly, it is possible that there is another cause different than radiation.  The one thing that I would say it is almost certainly not is progression of prostate cancer.  It is very rare for men to have symptoms related to progression of prostate cancer and it is exceedingly rare for men do have a recurrence of prostate cancer within 1 year of treatment.If these symptoms are bothering you the 1st step I would recommend is increasing your Flomax dosing to twice daily.  I would take 1 dose in the morning and 1 dose in the evening and see if you notice improvement in your symptoms.  Most men tolerate this increase in dosing well, although there is a small chance he would get dizziness or lightheadedness due to a decrease in blood pressure.  If you experience those symptoms I would recommend returning to your baseline dose.If increasing the dose your Flomax does not help I would then recommend adding an anti-inflammatory if you are able to take one (such as ibuprofen).  There are small percentage of men who need to you steroids to help with these symptoms.Typically we see a returned to baseline (meaning your baseline after radiation) within 4-6 weeks of these symptoms developing.  Once again, this is a benign process and not related to any progression of prostate cancer.Please let me know if you have any additional questions or concerns.  If you do end up taking the increased dose of Flomax, let me know and I will update your prescription accordingly.Best, [Name]

Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for your message. The 4-month ADT (Eligard) prescription was chosen based on your specific case and the current guidelines for intermediate-risk prostate cancer. This duration is often sufficient to enhance the effectiveness of radiation therapy while minimizing long-term side effects.

Regarding your urinary symptoms, it is not uncommon to experience changes in urination post-treatment. The symptoms you describe, such as weak flow and leakage, can be managed with medications like Flomax. If you have finished your current supply, we can renew your prescription.

As for the smoker's cancer questionnaire, it is a routine part of our follow-up process to ensure we have the most accurate and up-to-date information.

Please let me know if you have any further questions or concerns.

Best regards, Dr. [Name]

Mr. [Name]. Thank you for the note. We can certainly address those questions at your upcoming visit. There are a variety of factors that are used to determine the planned length of hormone therapy which vary from patient to patient. Once again we can review the specifics that led to a 4 month duration in your case.I have ordered a uroflow test to be done on Wednesday. This test will measure your urinary flow rate and how well you empty your bladder. This should help give us some context for your urinary function and assist with determining how we approach it. You should try to have a full bladder for that procedure, but not an overly full bladder, as that can skew the results. That appointment should be added to your appointment guide early next week. Best, [Name]

MessageNote: Last June, I had proton beam radiation for prostate cancer .  Recently, I have spotted blood from the rectum and went to see my family dr.  She mentioned there is thinning of the tissue as a result of radiation and put me on hydrocortisone ac for seven days and a tube also of hydrocortisone.  I have not had any spotting since and have one day left of the hydrocortisone ac. Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your concerns. It is not uncommon to experience rectal bleeding and tissue thinning as a side effect of radiation therapy, known as radiation proctitis. The treatment with hydrocortisone you received is appropriate and it's good to hear that it has resolved the spotting.

While radiation proctitis can be a recurring issue, many patients find that symptoms improve over time. It is important to continue monitoring your symptoms and follow up with your scheduled colonoscopy in [Month]. If you experience any new or worsening symptoms, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

Hi Mr. [Name],You can potentially develop blood in the urine or blood in the stool, months to years after prostate cancer radiation treatment.  It typically resolves on its own, but Dr. [Name] recommends you notify us if this occurs and be seen here in [Location].  He also prefers that you undergo a colonoscopy here at [Location] Clinic, because we would not want you to undergo unnecessary rectal biopsies, for example.  Reply back if you wish to have that arranged here.  Also, let me know which pharmacy I can call the bowel prep in to. Let me know if you have any questions.
Thanks, [Name]

MessageNote: Hello,Had 6 month-45mg injection [Time]. * Experiencing extreme fatigue daily (in bed 12 hrs +/- per day, trouble walking very far, doing simple tasks, etc. rest of time).* Almost continuous headache (finally took tylenol today- helped somewhat)* Trouble breathing both resting and during exertion (asthmatic, but symptoms generally respond to medication much better than what is occurring currently)* Cloudy thinking* Feelings of depression about my medical condition/inability to accomplish thingsI do not remember the intensity and/or longevity of these effects with previous injections.Had an acupuncture session yesterday that helped with asthma symptoms (more so during resting than exertion), but continuous extreme fatigue ongoing as well as headache off & on.* Following herbs prescribed- Si Jun Zi Tang, Che Qian Ze, Tao Ren. ANY CONTRAINDICATIONS WITH PROSTATE CANCER TREATMENT?Instead of waiting for our upcoming video conference, thought you should know the above now.Is there anything I can do to alleviate above. It is a miserable existence now...Thank you- [Name]
Response.1:
Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out regarding the symptoms you are experiencing after your recent Eligard injection. It is not uncommon to experience fatigue, headaches, and trouble breathing as side effects of Eligard. Here are some recommendations to help manage these symptoms:

Fatigue: Ensure you are getting adequate rest and try to maintain a regular sleep schedule. Light physical activity, such as short walks, can sometimes help improve energy levels.
Headache: Continue using Tylenol as needed. Ensure you stay hydrated and avoid any known headache triggers.
Trouble Breathing: Since you have a history of asthma, continue using your prescribed asthma medications. If symptoms persist or worsen, please seek immediate medical attention.
Cloudy Thinking and Depression: These can be side effects of hormonal therapy. Consider discussing these symptoms with a mental health professional for additional support.
Regarding the herbs you are taking (Si Jun Zi Tang, Che Qian Ze, Tao Ren), there are no known contraindications with your prostate cancer treatment. However, it is always best to discuss any herbal supplements with your healthcare provider.

If your symptoms do not improve or if you have any further concerns, please do not hesitate to reach out.

Best regards, [Name], M.D., Ph.D.

Mr. [Name],We would recommend you discuss these symptoms with your primary care provider.  Some of what you are describing would not be from an Eligard injection.  Eligard should not cause "extreme" fatigue, continuous headaches, or trouble breathing, for example.  Please do check in with your local internists or primary care provider.  Thanks, [Name]

MessageNote: Hello Dr. [Name]-Experiencing severe, long term anemia, with no real resolution of problem, including iron infusions. No one is able to determine cause. I've attached more recent blood reports (with low RBC, hemoglobin, hematocrit, iron, etc, values) and a bone marrow biopsy (including trace/no Fe storage in marrow) , for reference. Probably a bit of a reach, but is there any possibility previous Eligard injections or proton therapy for prostate cancer would have any bearing on this problem?
Response.1:
Hello Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out regarding your concerns about anemia. Based on the information provided, it is unlikely that your previous Eligard injections or proton therapy for prostate cancer are the direct cause of your long-term anemia. Eligard and proton therapy typically do not have long-term effects on red blood cell production or iron storage. However, I recommend discussing this further with your hematologist to explore other potential causes and treatment options.

Best regards,
[Name]

Hello Mr. [Name],You just completed androgen deprivation therapy in [Month] according to our records.  It can thus take 3 to 12 months for testosterone to recover.   I see that your recent hemoglobin level is 12.6.   This is not bad given the Eligard use and your age.   I understand you may be experiencing significant fatigue but I wouldn't attribute it solely to the anemia.  Given your history of androgen deprivation therapy, the bone marrow biopsy may not have been warranted given your modest decrease in hemoglobin level.   If the hemoglobin were to be below 10 then I might start to suspect there is an underlying cause beyond the Eligard.The amount of radiation you received was minimal, less than 3 to 5% of the bone marrow was radiated.  One study I recently read demonstrated that at 25 months following completion of androgen deprivation therapy hemoglobin levels between untreated patients and treated patients was indistinguishable.   But, it should be noted that most people acclimate to having a hemoglobin level one to two points below normal.We can discuss further by telephone if you'd like.Hope this helps.   You should gradually feel better as the effects of androgen deprivation therapy wears off.   I'd not also that your bone marrow biopsy shows red blood cell production.Sincerely, [Name]

MessageNote: There was an online discussion about an advanced prostate cancer detection test called Parsortix.  This test detects circulating tumor cells in the blood.  The only problem is that the prostate cancer has already metastasized at that point.  At what Gleason score level, (and what point) will prostate cancer begin to metastasize and get into the blood?  And where is early prostate cancer detection at currently other than using just rising PSA scores? Thanks.  You'll be getting my new PSA score in a week or so. [Name] [Phone Number]
Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for your inquiry about the Parsortix test and early prostate cancer detection methods. The Parsortix test detects circulating tumor cells in the blood, which typically indicates that the prostate cancer has metastasized. Prostate cancer can metastasize at various Gleason scores, but it is more common at higher scores (Gleason 7 and above).

For early detection, we currently rely on PSA testing, digital rectal exams, and MRI scans. These methods help us monitor the prostate and detect any changes early on.

Please continue with your regular PSA testing and follow-up visits as scheduled. If you have any new symptoms or concerns, feel free to discuss them during your follow-up visits.

Best regards, Dr. [Name]

There is no cut-off point of Gleason score that begins to metastasize. Even Gleason 3+3 cancer can metastasize.There is no other approved test that can be used routinely for early detection of prostate cancer recurrence.Dr. [Name]

MessageNote: Good morning.  I am having some difficulty maintaining an erection post radiation.  Can you prescribe a medication to help pe our pre treatment discussion?  Let me know.  Thanks. [Name]
Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out regarding your concern. Erectile dysfunction can indeed be a side effect following radiation therapy for prostate cancer. Based on our pre-treatment discussion, I would recommend starting with an over-the-counter option such as sildenafil (Viagra) or tadalafil (Cialis). These medications can help improve erectile function.

Please start with the following:

Sildenafil (Viagra): Take 50 mg approximately one hour before sexual activity. Do not take more than one dose in 24 hours.
Tadalafil (Cialis): Take 10 mg at least 30 minutes before sexual activity. The effect can last up to 36 hours. Do not take more than one dose in 24 hours.
If you find that these medications are not effective or if you experience any side effects, please let us know so we can discuss alternative treatments.

Feel free to contact our office if you have any further questions or concerns.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

MessageNote: Does the Proton Therapy I received hit all the prostate mass or is it targeted to specific areas of prostate based on MRI's and Biopsy? [Name]
Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for your question regarding the targeting of proton therapy. The proton therapy you received is designed to target the entire prostate gland, including any areas identified by MRI and biopsy. This approach ensures that the entire prostate is treated while minimizing exposure to surrounding tissues.

Best regards, [Name], M.D., Ph.D.

Hi Mr. [Name],The proton radiation (and actually all prostate radiation that we offer!) targets the entire prostate. There is a lower chance of prostate cancer coming back if we treat the entire prostate, instead of just part of the prostate. We know that small prostate cancer cells can exist even outside of the most suspicious areas/tumors that are often identified on MRI. This is the same reason that when doing biopsies, we take 12 random samples as well as the MRI-guided biopsy of the most suspicious lesion/tumor.Here is a screen shot from your treatment, showing that the high dose of radiation is going to the entire prostate, and staying almost completely out of the bladder and other nearby organs and bones.Hope you had a good day!

[Name], MD

Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your concerns. The side effects you are experiencing, including hot flashes, urination issues, reduced penis size and sexual interest, low energy level, and stamina, are common following androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) and radiation therapy. These side effects can persist for several months after treatment completion.

Hot Flashes: These are a common side effect of ADT. Over-the-counter options like cool compresses, wearing light clothing, and using fans can help. If they become severe, medications such as gabapentin or venlafaxine may be considered.

Urination Issues: Frequent urination and incomplete bladder emptying can occur post-radiation. Pelvic floor exercises and staying hydrated can help manage these symptoms. If they persist, medications like alpha-blockers (e.g., tamsulosin) may be beneficial.

Reduced Penis Size and Sexual Interest: These changes are also related to ADT. Open communication with your partner and exploring other forms of intimacy can be helpful. If sexual function is a significant concern, medications like sildenafil (Viagra) or tadalafil (Cialis) may be considered.

Low Energy Level and Stamina: Fatigue is a common side effect. Regular light exercise, maintaining a healthy diet, and ensuring adequate rest can help improve energy levels.

Please let us know if any of these symptoms worsen or if you have any other concerns. We are here to support you through your recovery.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

This means the medication would have stopped being released in your system [Month]. The purpose of the medication was to wipe out your testosterone but with that unfortunately patients can experience adverse symptoms such as you're experiencing. Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out. The symptoms you are experiencing, such as hot flashes, nausea, sore breasts, and low energy, are common side effects of androgen deprivation therapy (ADT). These side effects can persist for several months after the last injection. Typically, it can take 4-8 months for testosterone levels to start returning to normal, but this can vary from person to person.

In the meantime, here are some suggestions to help manage your symptoms:

Hot Flashes: Over-the-counter remedies like cool compresses, fans, and wearing light clothing can help. Some patients find relief with supplements like vitamin E or black cohosh, but please consult with your primary care provider before starting any new supplements.
Nausea: Eating small, frequent meals and avoiding spicy or fatty foods can help. Ginger tea or ginger candies may also provide relief.
Sore Breasts: Wearing a supportive bra and using over-the-counter pain relievers like ibuprofen can help manage discomfort.
Low Energy: Gentle exercise, such as walking or yoga, can help improve energy levels. Ensure you are getting adequate rest and staying hydrated.
If your symptoms persist or worsen, please let us know so we can explore other management options.

Best regards, Dr. [Name]

Regarding the hot flashes you are experiencing, these are a common side effect of the androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) you received alongside your radiation treatment. Hot flashes can indeed be quite uncomfortable, especially in warmer climates.

Typically, hot flashes can last for several months after the completion of ADT. For most patients, they gradually decrease in intensity and frequency over time. It is not uncommon for them to persist for up to a year or more, but they usually become more manageable as time goes on.

Stay Cool: Wear light, breathable clothing and use fans or air conditioning to keep your environment cool.

Over-the-Counter Remedies: Some patients find relief with over-the-counter supplements like vitamin E or soy products, though the evidence for their effectiveness is mixed.

MessageNote: I feel pretty good but Sunday and now Monday I'm experiencing significant headaches. Is that to be expected? Ibuprofen and Tylenol don't seem to take care of completely. Thanks. [Name]
Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out regarding your recent headaches. Headaches can sometimes occur after radiation therapy, although they are not very common. Since Ibuprofen and Tylenol are not providing complete relief, I recommend the following steps:

Hydration: Ensure you are well-hydrated, as dehydration can contribute to headaches.
Rest: Make sure you are getting adequate rest and sleep.
Over-the-Counter Options: You may try using a combination of Ibuprofen and Acetaminophen (Tylenol) together, as they can sometimes be more effective when used in combination. However, do not exceed the recommended dosage for either medication.
Monitor Symptoms: Keep a log of your headaches, noting their frequency, duration, and any other associated symptoms.
If your headaches persist or worsen, please let us know so we can explore other potential causes and treatments. We may consider prescribing a different medication or investigating further if necessary.
[Name]

Response.1: Mr. [Name], Good Morning. You received a 6 month hormone injection on [Time], this will expire in [Time]. It can take another 4-6 months for your testosterone to slowly recover, we anticipate side effects to subside over the next 4-6 months after your injection expires. Typically medications do not help with ED given your testosterone is depleted. Please let us as we can surly try medications after your injection expires in [Month]. [Name]

Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your concerns. Erectile dysfunction can be a side effect of radiation therapy, and it is not uncommon for patients to experience changes in erectile function during and after treatment. In many cases, these changes can improve over time, but it is also possible for some patients to experience longer-term effects.

There are several treatment options available to help manage erectile dysfunction, including:

Oral Medications: Medications such as sildenafil (Viagra), tadalafil (Cialis), and vardenafil (Levitra) can be effective in improving erectile function. These medications are typically taken before sexual activity.
Vacuum Erection Devices: These devices can help achieve and maintain an erection by creating a vacuum around the penis, drawing blood into the tissue.
Penile Injections: Medications can be injected directly into the penis to help achieve an erection.
Counseling: Psychological support and counseling can be beneficial, especially if emotional or psychological factors are contributing to the condition.
I recommend starting with oral medications, as they are often effective and easy to use. If you would like, I can prescribe one of these medications for you to try. Please let me know if you have any preferences or if you would like to discuss other treatment options.

Best regards, [Name]

Fatigue: It is common to experience some fatigue following radiation therapy. Ensure you are getting adequate rest and staying hydrated. Light exercise, such as walking, can also help manage fatigue.

Brain Fog and Dizziness: These symptoms can be concerning. Since you mentioned that the dizziness occurs with movement, it may be related to postural changes. Elevated Blood Pressure: Your recent blood pressure readings are higher than usual. This could be a side effect of the treatment or related to other factors. I recommend continuing to monitor your blood pressure daily. If it remains elevated, we may need to adjust your medications or explore other interventions.

Tamsulosin (Flomax): Since you have started taking Tamsulosin, please monitor for any side effects such as dizziness, especially when standing up quickly. Ensure you are drinking plenty of fluids and making slow position changes.

Please keep us updated on your symptoms and blood pressure readings. If you have any further questions or concerns, do not hesitate to reach out.

Good afternoon, Mr. [Name],I am sorry that you are experiencing these side effects. Going through your most recent history, it looks like you received your hormone injection on [Time]. Usually, the side effects start to build up after about a month after this medication being administered. The most common side effects include fatigue, decreased libido, and hot flashes. The fatigue and brain fog are likely related to this medication. In regard to your dizziness with changing positions, this could be related to the tamsulosin (Flomax) you take once each day. Flomax can DECREASE blood pressure; it does not raise blood pressure. Do you take metoprolol to control your blood pressure (per your chart, looks like you take this once a day)? It may be worthwhile to check in with your primary care provider for further evaluation of your blood pressure to determine possible increase or change in medications to help keep this better controlled.Please let us know any other questions you may have. Regarding your symptoms of frequent hot flashes and excessive perspiration, these are common side effects of androgen deprivation therapy (ADT). Here are a few strategies that might help manage these symptoms:
Stay cool by wearing light clothing and using fans or air conditioning.
Avoid triggers such as spicy foods, caffeine, and alcohol.
Consider over-the-counter remedies like vitamin E supplements or herbal supplements such as black cohosh, but please consult with your healthcare provider before starting any new supplements.

Response.1: Hello Mr. [Name], Having more frequent and looser bowel movements is an expected side effect from radiation as it can cause inflammation in that area. This should improve as the inflammation subsides. The slight blood on the toilet paper could be from irritation of wiping frequently. I would recommend trying a moist wipe (such as baby wipes) for awhile. Radiation can flare hemorrhoids, so I am not sure if you have a history of those or not but that could also be where the pink tinge is coming from. If the liquid stools continue you could try taking a 1/2- 1 tablet of Imodium (over the counter). Best, [Name]

Dear Mr. [Name],

The pink color on the toilet paper could be due to minor irritation from frequent wiping, especially if you have had enemas recently.

Based on your description, it does not sound like you have a significant infection, but it is important to monitor your symptoms. Here are a few recommendations:

Hydration: Ensure you are drinking plenty of fluids to stay hydrated.
Diet: Consider a bland diet for a few days to see if it helps settle your stomach.
Over-the-Counter Medication: You may use over-the-counter anti-diarrheal medications like loperamide (Imodium) if needed.

Response.1: [Name],Thank you for letting us know about your difficulties urinating. We would generally recommend starting a medication called Flomax (tamsulosin), taken once daily in the evenings. MessageNote: Good morning Dr [Name],It has been difficult for me to sleep during the night since having radiation, especially over the last 30 days.  Is there any thing you would suggest or prescribe to help?  I'm averaging about 4 hours of overnight sleep.Thank you, [Name]
Response.1:
Hi Mr. [Name], I'm sorry to hear that you're having difficulty sleeping. It is unlikely for this to be directly related to the radiation especially this far out from completion of treatment. The best things to try for improving your sleep are various lifestyle changes that help establish a conducive sleeping environment/pattern. A common medication to try over the counter would be melatonin and you could start with 5mg nightly if lifestyle changes alone do not help. If you continue to experience difficulty, it would be reasonable to discuss these concerns with you primary care provider. I have provided some additional information on insomnia below. Please let us know if you have additional questions. Best, [Name]

Insomnia Introduction
Our sleep needs vary. Most people need seven or eight hours of sleep a night to feel rested and function well during the day. Others need as little as six hours or as much as 10 hours of sleep to feel rested. Many people have occasional sleepless nights, but they can still function during the day.You have insomnia if you have difficulty sleeping on a regular basis. This regular lack of sleep may interfere with the ability to feel alert and rested during the day. Insomnia is usually a symptom; it is rarely a disease in itself. Symptoms of insomnia may include:Inability to get restful sleepDifficulty falling asleepWaking up too oftenWaking up too earlyWaking up feeling tired, even after a long period of sleepFatigue or sleepiness when awakeIrritabilityIf you have insomnia for longer than one month, talk to your health care provider about finding the cause and getting treatment.Diagnosis of InsomniaThe cause of insomnia can be hard to diagnose because so many things can affect sleep. Sometimes insomnia has no obvious cause.To diagnose the type and cause of your insomnia, your health care provider may ask you questions about the following:Your sleep patterns such as:How long and when you sleepHow long youâ€™ve had symptomsWhether the symptoms happen every nightWhether you snoreHow well you function during the dayIf you take medicationsIf you have health problemsIf you have a lot of stress in your lifeYour health care provider may ask you to fill out a questionnaire to help assess the level of your insomnia and its possible cause.Treatments for InsomniaYour health care provider may recommend making some behavior changes as the first step to eliminating your insomnia. Keep in mind what helps one person sleep better might cause very poor sleep for another. For example, some people sleep much better at night after a daytime nap; others sleep much worse. You are the best judge of what helps you sleep better.Suggestions for improving your sleepCut down on your time in bed -- Lying in bed awake for long periods can make your sleep less restful. Try spending one less hour or so in bed each day. It will be hard for two or three weeks, but gradually you may begin to sleep more soundly.Establish a regular time you go to bed and a regular time you get up -- Choose a time that is comfortable and convenient for you to go to bed each night and a time you will wake each day. Allow enough time to get the amount of sleep you need, but do not stay in bed longer than that.Avoid trying to sleep -- The more you lie in bed and try to sleep, the more awake you may become. If you have tried to fall asleep and, instead, become frustrated, get up and do something else. Read or watch television until you become drowsy and can fall asleep naturally.Avoid clock watching -- Set an alarm clock if needed, then put it under your bed. You may sleep better when you are not counting the minutes. Exercise -- Exercising during the day may help you sleep more soundly. However, exercise at least five to six hours before going to bed. Before beginning a new exercise routine, talk to your health care provider.Think about your sleep environment -- Some people need absolute darkness and use heavy drapes on the windows. Others need an open window. Some need constant noise such as an air conditioner or a sound machine. Some people sleep best when the room is cold; others, when it is warm. Try to keep pets out of the bedroom since they may wake you with noise or movement. Find out what helps you most. If youâ€™re not sleeping well, try changing the environment.Avoid alcohol -- Although alcohol can make you fall asleep, it will also cause you to wake up more times during the night once its sedative effect wears off.Limit caffeine intake -- Avoid all caffeine-containing beverages and products such as coffee, cola drinks, tea and chocolate. Do not eat or drink caffeine-containing products less than eight hours before you want to go to sleep. Avoid tobacco and nicotine -- Smoking disturbs sleep. Studies have shown smokers sleep more poorly than nonsmokers.Try to deal with your worries before bedtime -- If you often worry in bed, set aside 30 minutes during the day to address your worries. Write down your worries, think about each one and write down potential ways to solve the problems youâ€™re facing. Then try to set aside your worries before going to bed. If you find you have been trying to fall asleep and, instead, continue to worry, get up and try doing something else for awhile such as reading or watching television.Check your medications -- If you take medications, talk to your health care provider about whether they may be keeping you awake. Read the labels of over-the-counter products to see if they contain caffeine or other stimulants such as pseudoephedrine. Talk to a pharmacist if you are not sure which products are stimulants. Do not stop taking prescription medications unless your health care provider tells you to do so.Donâ€™t put up with pain -- Pain can affect your sleep. If pain is a problem, talk to your health care provider about the best way to manage your pain and lessen its effects on your sleep.Find ways to relax -- Relaxation techniques such as a warm bath three hours before bedtime, a gentle massage or listening to soothing music may help you sleep better.Other Treatments for InsomniaBesides the behavior change suggestions, some people with insomnia benefit from relaxation training, stress management classes or other forms of behavioral treatment. Others may benefit from treatment of their medical problem, while still others need counseling for managing day-to-day worries. MedicationSleeping pills can provide an occasional night of good sleep. They are generally used for a short period so you can sleep through a particularly hard time. Tell your health care provider if you are taking prescription medications, over-the-counter medications or supplements before taking sleeping pills.In the past, if you took sleeping pills for a long time, the medication may have stopped working after awhile. When you stopped taking the medication, your insomnia may have gotten worse for a few days or weeks. Newer forms of sleep medications are less likely to have these effects. If you are not sleeping better after improving your sleep habits, talk to your health care provider about sleep medications.When insomnia is caused by an illness, taking the medication for your condition may also help relieve insomnia. For example, antidepressants may help you sleep if you are depressed; pain medications may help if your insomnia is caused by pain.MelatoninMelatonin is a hormone produced in your brain, then released into your bloodstream in increasing amounts starting at dusk and tapering off toward morning. Over-the-counter melatonin supplements have been used for various medical conditions, including insomnia. Current research is inconclusive about how melatonin works and the potential long-term risks of taking it as a supplement. Talk with your health care provider before taking melatonin or other supplements.Note on using supplementsPeople often turn to supplements for many conditions. Most supplements have not been formally tested, and their effectiveness is unknown. Some supplements can interfere with other medications. To avoid possible toxic reactions to prescription medications, tell your health care provider if you are using a supplement -- especially if you are being treated with conventional therapies.If you have questions about insomnia or the information in this material, contact your health care provider. This material is for your education and information only. This content does not replace medical advice, diagnosis or treatment. New medical research may change this information. If you have questions about a medical condition, always talk with your health care provider.Â© 2006 Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research (MFMER). All rights reserved.MC1120-02rev1017

Dear Mr. [Name],

I understand that you have been experiencing difficulty sleeping since your radiation treatment. This is not uncommon and can be managed in several ways. Here are a few suggestions:

1. **Sleep Hygiene:** Ensure your sleep environment is quiet, dark, and cool. Avoid screens (TV, phone, computer) at least an hour before bedtime.
2. **Relaxation Techniques:** Consider relaxation exercises such as deep breathing, meditation, or gentle yoga before bed.
3. **Over-the-Counter Options:** Melatonin supplements can help regulate your sleep cycle. Start with a low dose (0.5-1 mg) about 30 minutes before bedtime.
4. **Prescription Options:** If over-the-counter options are not effective, we can consider a short-term prescription for a sleep aid.

Please let me know if you would like to try any of these options or if you have any other concerns.

Best regards,
Dr. [Name]

MessageNote: Hello Dr. [Name]:Receiving your letter was coincidental since I was just planning to write you with some follow-up questions I have been experiencing post proton therapy treatment.As further explanation of my motive, I am on the verge of having some follow-up tests for post mitral valve replacement and I want to present an accurate assessment to my cardiologist, Dr. [Name].I have been experiencing a few pronounced symptoms over the last few weeks:1) I am frequently very fatigued, which seems to be more or less depending on the day. There are days when I have very little energy and can't accomplish what I want or need to get done. Is this a normal reaction to the therapy or could it be a side effect from a medicine I'm taking? (Bicaludimide? I'm also taking lassix, potassium and metoprolol for the heart))2) I have gained about 5 lbs.since being on the medicine. Normal?3) From time to time, about once a day or so, I noticed that I have dark or rust-colored urine. There is no blood on the paper or floating in the bowl that I can see.4) I am experiencing more shortness of breath than before. Again, could this be a side effect from a medicine.Thanks for your response, Dr. [Name]
Response.1: Hi Mr. [Name], Thank you for reaching out with your questions. 1) Your testosterone blockers (leuprolide and bicalutamide) are almost certainly contributing to your fatigue. That is one of the primary side effects of those medications and having your testosterone levels low. There is no easy remedy besides trying to remain as active as you can. Once your testosterone therapy is complete, your energy will return again slowly.2) Weight gain is another associated side effect of these medications. Typically it is limited to 5-10 pounds; however, it is dependent on your activity levels as well.3) It is not unusual to have abnormal color urine after radiation. However, it should improve and resolve. It could be worth it to have your urine checked (urinalysis) as well as your kidney function (metabolic panel) just to be sure everything is okay. We can order this, or your local primary care provider should easily be able to test for you.4) Shortness of breath is not a side effect associated with prostate radiation nor with the testosterone-blocker medications. Have you had a recent respiratory infection? If not, and if it is persistent/worsening over several weeks (and not improving), it could be worth having your primary care provider investigate this further.Best, [Name]

Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your concerns. 

1. **Fatigue:** Fatigue can indeed be a side effect of bicalutamide, as well as other medications you are taking such as lassix and metoprolol. It is also not uncommon to experience some fatigue following radiation therapy. I recommend monitoring your energy levels and noting any patterns or triggers. If the fatigue becomes more severe or persistent, please let us know so we can further evaluate and adjust your treatment plan if necessary.

2. **Weight Gain:** Weight gain is a known side effect of bicalutamide. It is important to maintain a balanced diet and regular physical activity as much as possible. If you find the weight gain concerning or if it continues to increase, we can discuss potential adjustments to your medication or additional strategies to manage this side effect.

3. **Dark or Rust-Colored Urine:** The dark or rust-colored urine you are experiencing could be related to your medications or dehydration. It is important to stay well-hydrated. If the discoloration persists or if you notice any other symptoms such as pain or discomfort, please let us know so we can investigate further.

4. **Shortness of Breath:** Shortness of breath can be a side effect of your medications, particularly metoprolol, or it could be related to your cardiac condition. It is important to discuss this symptom with your cardiologist, Dr. David Foley, during your follow-up tests. If the shortness of breath worsens or becomes more frequent, please inform us immediately.

Best regards,
[Name]

Lymph Node Radiation: We typically target the specific lymph node that shows suspicious activity rather than the entire cluster. This approach helps to reduce side effects while effectively treating the cancer.

Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.

Best regards, [Name]

We drove to our fishing cabin yesterday (4 hours) and my side was aching so much I had to lay down. It feels like a gas bubble and groans and when I lay down it dissipates as gas.  The bulge (when there) is about 2" down and 2" to the left of my center tattoo. It feels like a kidney punch but in the front.  Last night someone said to me it might be related to my prostate treatment 4 months ago. Question: Do you know of any correlation? Question: Can you get me to see a specialist about this asap?Sincerely [Name]
Response.1: Hi Mr. [Name],My name is [Name], I'm a nurse practitioner working with Dr. [Name]. Thanks for reaching out regarding this issue, I'm sorry you're experiencing this. I reviewed your treatment plan, and there was no radiation that you received in your abdomen. Your radiation was delivered in the lower pelvis, away from where it sounds like you're experiencing these problems. We are not familiar with any radiation side-effect that sounds like what you're experiencing.On the other hand, what you describe very closely fits the description of what we'd expect from a hernia. Abdominal hernias tend to occur after significant straining (such as heavy lifting/pulling), get worse as the day goes on, and go away when lying down. However, of course, the diagnosis would require a physical exam by a general physician and possibly additional testing, outside of our specialty and expertise. Unfortunately we don't have any specialties at Mayo that would handle the work-up/diagnosis for an abdominal hernia - this is handled by primary/urgent/emergency care providers. If you're in such severe discomfort and can't wait until your appointment in August, I would suggest going in to urgent care or an emergency department to get an expedited work-up, at which point a diagnosis and possible surgical referral can be made. There is, of course, a possibility that it could be something else besides a hernia as well. But we do not feel this could be radiation-related.Thanks again for checking with us about this. Please let us know if you have any other questions.Best, [Name]

Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your concerns. Based on your description, it is possible that you may have a hernia, especially given the bulge and its behavior throughout the day. While it is less likely that this is related to your recent prostate radiation treatment, it is important to rule out any potential complications.

I recommend that you see a specialist as soon as possible for further evaluation. I will arrange for a referral to a general surgeon who can perform a thorough examination and potentially order imaging studies to confirm the diagnosis.

In the meantime, please try to avoid heavy lifting and strenuous activities that may exacerbate your symptoms. If you experience any severe pain or other concerning symptoms, please seek immediate medical attention.

Sincerely,

Dr. [Name]

Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for sharing your recent PSA test result. A PSA level of <0.1 is excellent news and indicates that your treatment continues to be effective. I am glad to hear that you are feeling great. Please continue with your regular follow-up and PSA monitoring as recommended.
