Mr. [Name], Good Morning. Thank you for the update, your PSA results look great. Please continue to have your PSA checked every 6 months. [Name]

MessageNote: Hello, I am having issues with going to the bathroom both urinating and number two. I feel like I have to go frequently but am not able to fully be empty/fully going. The medicine they put me on over the weekend seems to be helping a little bit but still not fully able to go. I have also been taking miralax. Feeling fatigued and weaker than normal. Are these all common side effects? What other side effects are there that I can expect? What other things can I be doing to help these?Thank you .[Name]
Response.1:
Dear [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your concerns. The symptoms you are experiencing, such as frequent urination, incomplete emptying, and fatigue, are common side effects of radiation therapy for prostate cancer. Here are some details and recommendations:

Frequent Urination and Incomplete Emptying:
Flomax: Continue taking Flomax as prescribed. It can take a few days to a week to see the full effect. If you do not notice improvement, we can consider increasing the dose to 0.8 mg.
Hydration: Ensure you are drinking plenty of fluids, but try to avoid caffeine and alcohol as they can irritate the bladder.
Bladder Training: Try to schedule bathroom visits every 2-3 hours to help train your bladder.
Bowel Movements:
Miralax: Continue taking Miralax as needed to help with bowel movements.
Diet: Increase your intake of fiber-rich foods such as fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. This can help with bowel regularity.
Hydration: Drink plenty of water to help with bowel movements.
Fatigue and Weakness:
Rest: Ensure you are getting enough rest. Fatigue is a common side effect of radiation therapy.
Light Exercise: Gentle activities like walking can help improve energy levels.
Nutrition: Maintain a balanced diet to support your overall health.
If your symptoms persist or worsen, please let us know. We can adjust your treatment plan as needed to help manage these side effects.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

Hello Mr. [Name], Thank you for messaging in. We are sorry to hear you are experiencing radiation related side effects. Urinary or bowel frequency/urgency, difficulty emptying your bowel or bladder, burning while urinating, and fatigue are all potential side effects of radiation therapy. Typically side effects improve within 2-3 weeks post radiation therapy. To manage fatigue, we recommend light activity with extra rest. To manage urinary frequency/urgency or difficulty emptying your bladder, we recommend taking 1 tablet of Flomax with supper. If 1 tablet is insufficient, you may increase to 2 tablets per day. If urinary symptoms are most bothersome overnight, you may take 2 tablets of Flomax with supper. If urinary symptoms are bothersome day and night, you may take 1 tablet with breakfast and 1 tablet with supper. Additionally we recommend staying well hydrated and taking Ibuprofen (600 mg 3 times a day) and/or Tylenol (1000 mg 3 times a day) if you are experiencing discomfort while urinating. Typically an NSAID like Ibuprofen is most helpful if you are able to take NSAIDS. Lastly, I would recommend taking a fiber supplement such as Metamucil or increasing the fiber in your diet to bulk up your stools to better empty. I will include a list of high fiber foods below. If you are experiencing bowel frequency/urgency or diarrhea, please cut back on MiraLAX and instead take only Metamucil. Please try the above interventions and we will touch base again on Wednesday during our management visit. Thanks, Brooke, RNRadiation Oncology Increasing Fiber in Your DietWhat Is Fiber?Fiber is made up of all parts of plant foods that your body does not digest or absorb. It is commonly called â€œroughageâ€ or â€œbulk.â€ On nutrition labels, it is measured in grams (g).Eating fiber helps you:Keep your stool soft.Increase the weight and size of your stool.Use less pressure to move stool through your colon.These benefits may help people manage certain medical conditions, such as constipation, diverticulosis, hemorrhoids, diarrhea, fecal incontinence, and irritable bowel syndrome.Fiber also may help lower your risk for certain types of cancer, heart disease and type 2 diabetes. And it may help you manage your weight.Note: Talk to a dietician if you have more than one medical condition that is affected by what you eat and drink. Examples of this type of medical condition include constipation, diabetes, obesity, pregnancy, irritable bowel syndrome, and gastroparesis. A visit with a dietician may help you reach your dietary goals with fewer side effects.How much fiber is enough?Every person is different. The right amount of fiber is different for each person. Eating more than 50 grams of dietary fiber per day does not help most people. In fact, it may cause pain or bloating in the abdomen.The suggested amounts of fiber for most people are shared here:Daily fiber goal for women:50 years and older: 21 gUnder 50 years: 25 gDaily fiber goal for men:50 years and older: 30 gUnder 50 years: 38 gTwo types of dietary fiberThere are two types of dietary fiber, soluble and insoluble. Both are found in plant foods. Both help prevent constipation.Soluble fiber may help lower cholesterol. Good sources include oats, barley and some fruits and vegetables. Fruits include oranges, grapefruit, peaches, and plums. Vegetables include carrots, broccoli, navy or kidney beans, and peas.Insoluble fiber makes the intestines work better. It increases the amount of stool you have. Wheat bran, whole-grain breads and cereals, and brown rice have high levels of this fiber. Some fruits and vegetables also have high levels. Fruits include apples and pears with their skin on. Vegetables include broccoli and spinach.Sources of FiberIt is very important that you eat a variety of high-fiber foods every day. Different kinds of fiber help the body in different ways.The following table lists some foods high in fiber. It also shows the amount of fiber in a serving. This list does not include all sources of fiber. Be sure to read food nutrition labels to find other high-fiber foods. See Figure 1.Food Group 2 to 3 grams of fiber per serving 4 to 6 grams of fiber per serving More than 6 grams of fiber per serving Grain productsOne serving of a grain product is 1/2 cup or 1 average piece unless otherwise listed.Eat 3 or more servings per day from this food group. Amaranth, cookedBarley, cookedBread or roll, 100% whole-wheatCheeriosâ„¢, 1 cupCrackers, whole- grain, such as Triscuitâ„¢ (6) or Wheat Thinsâ„¢ (16)Crispbread, whole- grain (for example, Wasaâ„¢), 1 pieceMuesliOat bran cereal, cookedOatmeal, cooked Popcorn, popped, 2 to 3 cupsQuinoa, cooked Rice, brown or wild, cooked, 1 cupRice bran, 2 tablespoons Wheat bran, 2 tablespoonsWheat germ, 2 tablespoonsSpaghetti or macaroni, whole-wheat, cooked Bulgur, cookedGrape-Nut Flakesâ„¢, 1 cupHominy, cookedQuaker Oat Squaresâ„¢, 1 cupWheat flakes (for example, Wheatiesâ„¢ and Totalâ„¢), 1 cup Bran flakes, 1 cupChexâ„¢ cereal, wheat or multi-bran, 1 cupCorn Branâ„¢, 1 cupGrape-Nutsâ„¢Kashi GOâ„¢All-Branâ„¢Fiber Oneâ„¢Bran Budsâ„¢Raisin bran cereal, 1 cupShredded wheat cereal, 1 cup of the bite-size cereal or 2 biscuits of the whole- biscuit-size cereal Vegetables and fruitsOne serving of a vegetable or fruit is 1/2 cup cooked, 1 cup raw, or 1 medium piece unless otherwise listed.Eat 5 or more servings per day from this food group. BroccoliBrussels sproutsCarrotsCauliflower, rawCorn, 1 ear (8 inches or 1/2 cup)Green beansGreensKohlrabi, rawParsnipsPeas, snow or sugar snapPotatoesPumpkinRutabagaSauerkrautSpinachSquash, butternutSweet potatoesTomato, 1 largeTomato, cooked or cannedTomato sauce or pasteWax beansApricots, 3 to 4 freshBananaBlueberriesCherriesDried fruit (for example, figs, apricots and prunes), 1/4 cupFigs, cannedFigs, 2 freshGuavaKiwiMangoNectarineOrangePapayaPeachPears, cannedPrune juice, 1 cupStrawberries Baked beans Dried beans (for example, navy, pinto, kidney, garbanzo, lima, soybeans), cooked or cannedPeas, green Squash, acornVegetable-protein burger, varies (check label)ApplePear, freshPrunes, cooked or canned Artichokes, French or globeAvocadoLentils, cookedDried peas, cookedBlackberriesRaspberries Nuts and seeds One serving of nuts or snack seeds is 1/4 cup unless otherwise listed. Flax seeds, 1 tablespoonPeanuts and other nutsPeanut butter and other nut butters, 2 tablespoonsPumpkin or squash seeds, roastedSoybeans (soy nuts), roastedSunflower seed kernels Chia seeds, 1 tablespoon  Fiber supplementsIt is best to try to get most of your fiber from food because food offers other nutrients too. However, if you are not able to eat as much fiber as you need in a typical day, talk to your health care provider about using a fiber supplement. Examples are Citrucelâ„¢, Fiberconâ„¢, Benefiberâ„¢, and Metamucilâ„¢.Fiber is sometimes added to foods like yogurt and fiber bars. The most common types of added fibers are chicory root, inulin, pectin, and fructooligosaccharides (FOS). Added fibers are not as helpful for constipation as the natural dietary fiber found in foods. Also, when you consume more than 3 grams of one of these added fibers per serving, you may have abdominal bloating and more gas than you usually have.Increasing Your FiberIdeas for adding fiber to your dietIt is important to add fiber to your diet slowly. You may add a few grams each week. If you add too much fiber too fast, you may have bloating, abdominal pain, more gas than you usually have, or all of these.Read the â€œNutrition Factsâ€ label to find out the amount of dietary fiber in a food. Foods with 2 to 3 grams of dietary fiber per serving are good sources of fiber. Foods with 4 or more grams of dietary fiber per serving are excellent sources of fiber. See Figure 1.Choose bread with whole-grain flour listed as the first ingredient on the nutrition label. Breads with visible seeds and grains are an excellent choice.Eat cereals with bran in the name. These usually have the most fiber. If you prefer the taste of a lower- fiber cereal, mix it with some bran cereal to get more fiber. You also can add 2 to 3 tablespoons of wheat germ or ground flaxseed to hot or cold cereals. Add bran cereal, wheat germ or ground flaxseed to baked products. Examples include meatloaf, breads, muffins, casseroles, cakes, and cookies.Use bran, wheat germ or flax as a crunchy topping for casseroles, salads, yogurt, and cooked vegetables.Substitute whole-grain flours for white flour in baking. As a general rule, if a recipe calls for 1 cup of white flour, you can use 1/2 cup of whole-wheat flour and 1/2 cup of white flour instead.Try to eat more dried peas, beans and lentils. Add them to main dishes, soups and salads. For food safety, be sure to soak and cook as the label tells you.Use brown rice, bulgur wheat, barley, quinoa, and whole-wheat pasta in soups and casseroles or as side dishes.See also â€œAvoiding Extra Gas When You Add Fiber.â€ If you have questions about side effects, talk to your health care provider.A note about snack foodsIf you snack once in a while, think about choosing foods that are higher in fiber.Most common snack foods, such as candy, cookies and potato chips, are made of manufactured ingredients that are not good for you. This type of food is called refined. Refined foods have little or no fiber. And they usually donâ€™t satisfy hunger unless you eat a lot of food. This leads to extra calories and may lead to weight gain.To avoid unhealthy snacking, make a plan to snack on good, higher-fiber foods.Choose fresh or dried fruit, raw vegetables, nuts, or popcorn for snacks. Eat a minimum of 5 servings of fruit and vegetables a day.Choose bran muffins or granola bars instead of donuts and desserts. High-Fiber Foods Sample MenuBreakfast1 orange1 cup shredded wheat1 slice whole-wheat toast1 teaspoon margarine1 cup skim milk1 cup coffee or tea Dietary fiber (grams)362000 Noon meal2 ounces sliced turkey2 slices whole-wheat bread1 teaspoon mayonnaise1 cup carrot sticks1 cup coffee or tea 04030 Evening meal3 ounces baked chicken1 baked potato, medium1/2 cup green beans2 teaspoons margarine1 pear1 cup skim milk1 cup coffee or tea 0220500 Snack3 cups popcorn1 cup cranberry juice 30 Total grams dietary fiber: 30 More tips about a high-fiber dietDrink eight, 8-ounce glasses of fluid each day. Fiber absorbs liquid in your intestines. So you need enough liquid to form a soft, bulky stool.Eat your meals on a regular schedule. Eat some fiber-rich food at each meal. This helps you have healthy bowel function.Include exercise in your daily routine, as directed by your health care provider. Exercise also helps you have healthy bowel function.Avoiding Extra Gas When You Add FiberSome high-fiber foods are more likely to cause gas. Choose from the following foods to help you limit gas while you increase your fiber intake.Fruits Vegetables Grains Nuts ApricotsBananasBerriesCherriesFigs, fresh or cannedGuavKiwiMangosNectarineOranges, freshPapayaPeaches, freshPlums, freshTangerines, fresh AvocadoBamboo shootsBeans, green or waxBeetsCarrotsCornGreensOkraPeas, greenPotatoesSweet potatoesPumpkinSpinachSquash, winterTomatoes BarleyBread, 100% whole- wheatCereals, whole- grain, such as:- Amaranth- Cheeriosâ„¢- Corn bran- Granola- Grape- Nutsâ„¢- Grape-Nuts Flakesâ„¢- Kashiâ„¢- Muesli- Oatmeal- Oat bran- Oat squares- Quinoa- Rice bran- Shredded wheat- Totalâ„¢- Wheat Chexâ„¢- Wheat germ- Wheatiesâ„¢Crackers, whole- grainFlat breads and crisp breads, whole-grain, (for example, Wasa)Pasta, whole-wheatPopcornRice, brown or wild NutsNut buttersSunflower seeds and other snack seedsFlax seeds Foods more likely to cause gasApples, apple juiceBranBroccoliBrussels sproutsCabbage, sauerkrautCauliflowerDried beansLentilsLima beansOnionsPrunes, prune juiceRaisinsTo reduce the gassy effect of these foods, eat them in small amounts, such as a half-portion. And try to eat them in a different form, such as cooked instead of raw.If you get gas or bloating, these symptoms may go away as you become used to your new higher-fiber diet. Talk with your health care provider if your symptoms continue to bother you.Other tips for less bloating and gasEat and drink slowly.Avoid or limit carbonated drinks.Avoid using straws and avoid drinking from bottles that have narrow openings. Pour the drink into a cup instead.Limit the amount of fatty food you eat.Exercise regularly.Manage your stress.Final ThoughtsIncreasing your daily fiber has many health benefits. Your care team hopes you choose foods with good fiber content every day. If you are not consistent -- if you increase your fiber only once in a while -- it could lead to side effects that are bothersome.For more informationIf you have questions after you read this information, talk to your health care provider or a dietitian.This material is for your education and information only. This content does not replace medical advice, diagnosis or treatment. New medical research may change this information. If you have questions about a medical condition, always talk with your health care provider.Â© 2020 Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research (MFMER). All rights reserved.MC0884rev0220

MessageNote: I finished my radiation on [Time] and have had a terrible time with constipation since then. I have taken senna , increased my fluid intake and increased my fiber intake.What would you recommend next? [Name]
Response.1: Mr. [Name], I am sorry to hear you have been experiencing constipation. Typically we see the opposite, loose stools after radiation therapy. I would recommend taking 1-2 senna up to 2 times a day in combination with MiraLAX twice daily. If you continue to experience constipation, you may pick up an over the counter enema. In addition, continue a high fiber diet (fruits, veggies, whole grain foods, beans, peanuts, popcorn, etc.), drink lots of fluids, and walk frequently. I hope this helps. Thanks, [Name]

Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out regarding your constipation. I understand that you have already tried senna, increased your fluid intake, and increased your fiber intake. Here are a few additional recommendations that may help:

Over-the-Counter Options:
Polyethylene Glycol (MiraLAX): This is an osmotic laxative that can help soften stools and make them easier to pass. Follow the instructions on the package for dosing.
Docusate Sodium (Colace): This is a stool softener that can help make bowel movements more comfortable.
Lifestyle Modifications:
Physical Activity: Gentle exercise, such as walking, can help stimulate bowel movements.
Regular Meals: Eating at regular intervals can help regulate your digestive system.
If these measures do not provide relief, please let us know. We can discuss other options, including prescription medications if necessary.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

MessageNote: Are there any restrictions on having a colonoscopy with prostate radiation treatment? My gel spacer was placed the first week of July and my radiation was completed on August 11th.Thank you. [Name]
Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for your message. Given that your radiation treatment was completed on [Time] and your gel spacer was placed in early [Month], there should be no restrictions on having a colonoscopy at this time. The gel spacer and the radiation treatment should not interfere with the procedure. However, it is always a good idea to inform your gastroenterologist about your recent treatments so they can take any necessary precautions.

Best regards,
Dr. [Name]

Hi Mr. [Name],We would recommend that you wait at least 6 months after your last radiation treatment to have a colonoscopy done. That would put you to wait until after [Time]. Your gel spacer should be completely gone by then.Kind regards,[Name]

MessageNote: It's been almost 4 months since I received the hormone injection and the side effects I'm experiencing (mostly the flashes which have caused insomnia and the fatigue,headaches) don't seem to be lessening. Is this normal? Should I be concerned?How much longer should I expect to feel this way? [Name]
Response.1:
Dear [Name],
Thank you for reaching out regarding the side effects you are experiencing from the hormone injection (Leuprolide). It is not uncommon for side effects such as hot flashes, insomnia, fatigue, and headaches to persist for several months after the injection. These symptoms can be quite bothersome, but they are generally expected with hormone therapy.

Here are a few recommendations to help manage these side effects:

Hot Flashes:
Try to keep cool by wearing light clothing and using fans.
Avoid triggers such as spicy foods, caffeine, and alcohol.
Consider over-the-counter remedies like vitamin E or soy supplements, but please consult with your primary care provider before starting any new supplements.
Insomnia:
Maintain a regular sleep schedule.
Create a relaxing bedtime routine.
Avoid caffeine and electronic devices before bedtime.
Fatigue:
Ensure you are getting adequate rest.
Engage in light physical activity, such as walking, to boost energy levels.
Stay hydrated and eat a balanced diet.
Headaches:
Over-the-counter pain relievers like ibuprofen or acetaminophen can be helpful.
Ensure you are staying hydrated and managing stress.
If these symptoms continue to be severe or if you have any new concerns, please let us know. We can discuss additional management strategies or consider a referral to a specialist if needed.

Best regards,

[Name]

Hi Mr. [Name], The hormone therapy injection you received was a 4-month injection, so the medication should just be expiring about now (as it looks like you received it on [Time]). That being said, the side effects from hormone therapy will take time to resolve as your testosterone gradually increases. It may take several months after your hormone therapy injection expires for the side effects to improve. The length of time for improvement varies among patients, but we are hopeful that you should see some improvement over the coming months now that the medication is expiring as your testosterone begins to rebound and recover.Please let us know if you have any other questions or concerns. Thanks, [Name]

MessageNote: Hi Dr. [Name]. Tamsulosin dosage:  [Time] was the 4th of the five radiation treatments.  Early on, starting [Time] there was enough of a slow down in urine stream that I doubled the tamsulosin from .4 mg once a day to taking it twice a day.  This seems to have helped but it will cause me to run out early.  I very recently refilled a 90 day supply so there is no  immediate concern for running out.  Would you please submit a reinforcing prescription request?  My existing one is filled by Walgreens, [Location]?   Follow-up question:  Will this need to be a permanent increase in dosage or can I anticipate going back to just one pill a day after treatment side affects settle down?Back to side affects:  I still do notice a weaker stream at times.  Urine frequency and urgency is a bit more often.  There is some very mild burning when I urinate.  So far (crossing fingers) that is it.  Radiation team members have all been very sensitive, compassionate, responsive, informative, and appreciated by me. [Name]
Response.1:
Hi [Name],

Thank you for reaching out. I understand that you have been experiencing some urinary symptoms and have increased your tamsulosin dosage to twice a day. It is good to hear that this adjustment has been helping you.

Prescription Refill: I will submit a prescription request for tamsulosin to be filled at Walgreens, [Location]. This should ensure you have enough medication.

Dosage Adjustment: Regarding the dosage, it is common for urinary symptoms to increase during radiation treatment. The increased dosage of tamsulosin is likely temporary. Once your treatment is complete and the side effects begin to settle down, we can reassess and potentially reduce the dosage back to once a day. Please monitor your symptoms and let us know if there are any changes.

Symptom Management: For the mild burning sensation during urination, ensure you are staying well-hydrated. If the burning sensation worsens or if you notice any other concerning symptoms, please let us know immediately.

Thank you for your kind words about our team. We are here to support you through your treatment.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]
Mr. [Name], thank you for letting us know. I've updated your prescription at Walgreens to reflect your two capsules taken daily.Best, [Name]

MessageNote: My 5th and final radiation treatment for prostate Cancer was [Time].  Today, for the first time I noticed bright red blood on tissue after wiping from bowel movement. I did not detect any blood in toilet. I have not been experiencing diarrhea but I have had small BMs 2 to 3 times a day, mostly soft.  I happen to be at Mayo today, Wed, for my wife. Should I be seen by your staff while here today. Otherwise a 3 hr drive. Phone is [Phone Number] if you prefer to call. Thank you, [Name]
Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your concerns. I am glad to hear that you are managing well with the radiation treatments.

Regarding your tamsulosin dosage, it is common to experience urinary symptoms during radiation therapy. Increasing the dosage to 0.4 mg twice a day is appropriate for managing these symptoms. I will submit a prescription request to Walgreens for a refill to ensure you do not run out of medication.

As for whether this increase will be permanent, it is likely that you will be able to return to the original dosage of 0.4 mg once a day after the side effects of the radiation treatment subside. We will monitor your symptoms closely and make adjustments as needed.

Please continue to stay hydrated and report any new or worsening symptoms. If you have any further questions or concerns, do not hesitate to contact us.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

Hi [Name], Thanks for letting us know. I'm sorry that you're experiencing this. I do not think we would need to see you. For bright red blood, there are two likely explanation - either hemorrhoids or proctitis. Please monitor it closely and let us know if the bleeding continues for more than a week or if you experience a significant amount of blood (bleeding in toilet and filling toilet). If it does continue, we could consider having an examination with a gastroenterologist for a scope to evaluate the rectum.Best, [Name]

MessageNote: Hi Dr [Name]. First,  You had indicated that I suspend taking food supplements with antioxidant properties like vitamin C, K2  as they tend to counter the results required from radiation.  At what point in time may I resume taking?  (FYI 5th and final Proton Radiation was on [Time]).Second, I continue to notice mild bright red appearing on tissue paper a couple times a day after bowel movements.  It appears fresh.  It is NOT thick or clot-like.  There is nothing in the toilet, just on the tissue paper.  Any guess on how long this should persist before I trouble you again?  (fyi, I have been having bowel movements around 4-5 times a day but nothing that I would call uncontrollable diarrhea.)
Response.1:
Hi [Name],

  You can resume taking your antioxidant supplements, including vitamin C and K2, starting from [Time]. This will ensure that the radiation treatment has had sufficient time to achieve its desired effects.

  Regarding the mild bright red blood on tissue paper after bowel movements, this is likely due to radiation proctitis or hemorrhoids. It is common to experience this for a few weeks post-treatment. If the bleeding persists beyond [Time] or worsens, please let us know.

  Best regards,
  Dr. [Name]

Hi [Name],You can resume your antioxidants a week after radiation is complete, so now should be fine. As far as the blood in stools, keep an eye on that. If it remains minimal, then it is most likely related to either hemorrhoids or proctitis (from radiation). Do you have a history of hemorrhoids? If it continues or worsens in the next 1-2 months, let us know. If it is from radiation-related proctitis (rectum inflammation), it should go away in the next few months as you heal from radiation. [Name]

MessageNote: Hi Dr. [Name],Ever since the silicone guard was placed in, I have been experiencing diarrhea. It is now close to three weeks. The norm is to have three bowel movements (over a 2-4 hour period) in the morning. Then repeat the next day. It was mentioned that this may occur but I am wondering for how long?Also, my understanding is that I will meet with you in person this coming Monday, which I am looking forward to! Happy Friday, [Name]
Response.1:
Hi [Name],

Thank you for reaching out. I'm sorry to hear that you have been experiencing diarrhea since the placement of the silicone guard. This can sometimes happen as your body adjusts to the new device. Here are a few steps you can take to manage your symptoms:

Hydration: Ensure you are staying well-hydrated by drinking plenty of fluids, especially water and oral rehydration solutions.
Diet: Consider following a bland diet (e.g., bananas, rice, applesauce, toast) to help reduce the frequency of bowel movements.
Over-the-Counter Medications: You may try over-the-counter anti-diarrheal medications like loperamide (Imodium) if you haven't already. Please follow the instructions on the package.
If your symptoms persist or worsen, we can discuss further interventions during your appointment on Monday. Please let me know if you experience any additional symptoms such as abdominal pain or fever.

Looking forward to seeing you on Monday.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

Good afternoon, I'm sorry for the delayed response to your message. I hope the therapists on the team passed along my message. This is an uncommon side effect of the spacer placement. It may continue to happen while the bowel "settles down" or as the spacer dissolves, which can take up to 6-9 months. If it is manageable, I would recommend continuing to watch for now. You could also try Imodium (start with a half tab) in the morning to see if it decreases the frequency of bowel movements. We can certainly discuss further on Monday. Dr. [Name]

Is there another product I can use?As follow up the urinary burning has subsided and the diarrhea has gotten better.Let me know your opinion as I have set up a doctorâ€™s appointment for tomorrow afternoon so I can have the area evaluated.Hope you are well and thanks again for a great experience. For the acute sensitivity and itching in the perineum and anal area, you might find relief using a topical hydrocortisone cream or a barrier cream like zinc oxide. I see that you have a doctor's appointment scheduled for tomorrow afternoon, which is a good step to have the area evaluated in person.

1. **Behavioral Techniques:** Try to distract yourself when you hear running water. Engaging in a different activity can help reduce the urgency.

Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out regarding your recent episodes of blood in your urine. Given your history of prostate cancer and radiation treatments, it is possible that the hematuria could be related to radiation-induced changes in your bladder. Radiation can sometimes cause inflammation or irritation in the bladder, leading to symptoms like blood in the urine.

However, it is also important to consider other potential causes. The fact that your ER visit did not reveal any clear cause is reassuring, but we should continue to monitor the situation closely.

Here are a few steps you can take:

Hydration: Ensure you are drinking plenty of water to keep your urine diluted.
Avoid Bladder Irritants: Try to avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods, as these can irritate the bladder.
Monitor Symptoms: Keep track of any additional symptoms, such as pain, frequency of urination, or changes in urine color.
If the hematuria persists or worsens, or if you experience any new symptoms, please let us know immediately. We may need to conduct further investigations or refer you to a urologist for a more detailed evaluation.

Please feel free to reach out if you have any further questions or concerns.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

Mr. [Name] Thank you for the update, and I am sorry to hear that you are having blood in the urine.  I am going to get a copy of the CT scan images sent here for me to review.  However, it certainly seems that nothing concerning was noted.I do suspect this is at least partially related to the radiation last year.  As we noted at that time you are at a reasonably high risk of getting some bladder bleeding afterwards given that this was a 2nd course of radiation and the fact that you are on long-term warfarin.The 1 tests that would be needed to conclusively determine this would be a cystoscopy to evaluate the inner lining of your bladder.  That would be most helpful to rule out any other causes like a small bladder tumor that would potentially not be visible on a CT scan.  If it was determined that this cystoscopy that this was radiation cystitis causing the bleeding, there would be to options.  The 1st would be to simply observe it and hope that it improves over time.  This happens for about 50% of patients.  If observation is elected we typically tell patients not to let their bladder get overly distended and to avoid other things that can exacerbate bleeding such as heavy lifting, straining for bowel movements, or other high-level exertional activities.  If the bladder meeting were to persist and require intervention, the most helpful intervention is typically hyperbaric oxygen therapy.  This is a procedure where you sit in a high pressure, high oxygen chamber for approximately 1 hour per day for many weeks.  This helps facilitate healing of the damage tissue in the bladder.  While it is painless, it is a fairly large time commitment.  However, it is often effective at improving the bleeding.I will let you know once I have reviewed the CT scan images.  It would be reasonable to look into getting a cystoscopy done closer to home, or I would certainly be happy to arrange for 1 here at Mayo Clinic.  Alternatively, you could simply try to undertake the recommendations I gave you to avoid this and defer this cystoscopy until you have another episode.Best, [Name]

MessageNote: Good morning Dr [Name], Either I wasnâ€™t paying attention are missed it the explanation of possible side effects Iâ€™m wondering if you have any tips on lessening the severity of the pain from urination? Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out regarding the burning sensation you are experiencing with urination. This is a common side effect of radiation therapy to the prostate, as the treatment can cause inflammation and irritation of the urinary tract.

Here are some recommendations to help alleviate the discomfort:

Hydration: Drink plenty of water to help flush out your urinary system. Aim for at least 8-10 glasses of water a day.
Avoid Irritants: Try to avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods, as these can irritate the bladder and worsen symptoms.
Over-the-Counter Pain Relief: You may find relief with over-the-counter pain medications such as acetaminophen (Tylenol) or ibuprofen (Advil). Please follow the dosing instructions on the package.
Urinary Alkalizers: Products like sodium bicarbonate (baking soda) can help neutralize the acidity of your urine. Dissolve a teaspoon of baking soda in a glass of water and drink it once a day.
Warm Sitz Baths: Soaking in a warm bath for 15-20 minutes can help soothe the irritation.
The burning sensation may persist for a few weeks after the completion of your radiation therapy, but it should gradually improve. If the pain becomes unbearable or if you notice any other concerning symptoms, please do not hesitate to contact us.

We will continue to monitor your progress closely. Your next treatment is scheduled as planned, and we will reassess your symptoms during your next visit.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

Good afternoon Mr. [Name], Dr. [Name] recommends taking either Tylenol or Advil (ibuprofen) for pain relief. You can take 2 Advil two or three times a day during radiation. If you have been told not to take ibuprofen, you can use Tylenol instead. He also recommended drinking a small amount of cranberry juice to help with symptoms. (Not too much as it could interfere with your warfarin.)Symptoms are likely to worsen a bit during treatment and shortly after. After about a week post treatment, they should gradually subside. Let us know if you have any other questions. Thank you, [Name]

Response.1:
Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your questions. I understand that there is a lot of information to remember, and I am happy to clarify everything for you.

Dietary Restrictions:
Prohibited Beverages: No carbonated beverages, caffeinated beverages, citrus juice, alcohol, or decaf beverages.
Duration: These restrictions are in place for the duration of your treatment and for one week after your last radiation session.
Allowed Beverages: Apple juice, white grape juice, peppermint tea, and hot chocolate are allowed.
Fruits: Avoid citrus fruits. Other fruits are generally fine, but if you experience any discomfort, please let us know.
Side Effects:
Common Side Effects: Fatigue, frequent small bowel movements, burning sensation when urinating, and a thinner than usual urine stream.
Severe Side Effects: Inability to urinate, which requires immediate attention at the ER.
Duration: Side effects may worsen for a week after the last radiation session and then gradually subside over 4 to 6 weeks.
Enema Side Effects: Mild discomfort or cramping, usually lasting a few hours.
Water Intake:
Recommendation: Drink plenty of water to stay hydrated and help manage side effects. Given your cardiologists' advice, 64 fluid ounces a day should be sufficient. Please monitor your fluid retention and adjust as needed.
Beneficial Foods:
Probiotics: Foods high in probiotics, such as yogurt or kefir, can be beneficial.
Protein: Chocolate Ensure is fine for protein intake. Noodles in broth are also acceptable.
If you have any other questions or concerns, please do not hesitate to reach out. We are here to support you throughout your treatment.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]

MessageNote: I was thinking, with my PSA registering at 2.3 does that mean that the cancer is no longer in my body, or did it pass through or out of my body byway of my normal elimination of bodily  functions! Just wondering, maybe Iâ€™m over processing but my unrelenting inquiring mind wants to know!! [Name]
Response.1: Hi Mr. [Name], Thanks for your note. What actually happens post therapy is that these cancer cells die and are "eaten" up and recycled by the destruction organelles of the cell. Cancer cells are cancerous because they have lost their normal mechanism of halting their reproduction so essentially nothing is wrong with the individual parts of the cell, but rather than the cell does not turn off and reproduces inappropriately. Therefore, if you can destroy the cells (with radiation), the broken parts of the cell are recycled in a normal fashion by normal cellular waste mechanisms. Hopefully this helps to explain. Let me know of further questions. Best, Dr. [Name]

Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for your message. Your PSA level of 2.3 ng/mL is a positive sign and indicates that the treatment has been effective in reducing the activity of your prostate cancer. However, it does not necessarily mean that the cancer is completely gone. PSA levels are used to monitor the effectiveness of treatment and the status of the cancer over time. We will continue to monitor your PSA levels in future follow-up visits to ensure that the cancer remains under control.

If you have any further questions or concerns, please feel free to reach out.

Best regards, Dr. [Name]

MessageNote: Radiation cystitis is a complication of radiation therapy to pelvic tumors. The urinary bladder can be irradiated intentionally for the treatment of bladder cancer or incidentally for the treatment of other pelvic malignancies. Manifestations of radiation cystitis can range from minor, temporary, irritative voiding symptoms and painless, microscopic hematuria to more severe complications, such as gross hematuria; contracted, nonfunctional bladder; persistent incontinence; fistula formation; necrosis; and death. (See Presentation.)Cystoscopic view of a bladder showing the neovascuCystoscopic view of a bladder showing the neovascularity and telangiectasia of radiation cystitis.View Media GalleryTumors of the pelvic organs (ie, prostate, bladder, colon, rectum) are common in men, constituting 41% of expected new cancer diagnoses for 2021. [1] Radiation therapy is an important management tool for the treatment of these malignancies, creating significant potential for the development of radiation injury to the bladder.Radiation morbidity is due to incidental treatment of healthy organs. Efforts to reduce the complications of radiation have led to improvements in delivery mechanisms of radiation to the target organ. Wide-field treatment was the standard of care, but it. What I am essentially asking is that will there be any further need for another biopsy or Something else to Definitively determine what my situation is? [Name]

I have no doubt that there are number of interesting and potentially effective new treatments that are being considered for patients with arthritis.I would recommend reaching out to Dr. [Name] in Orthopedic surgery to see if he has any familiarity with this, as that is much closer to his area of Medicine.I hope you are doing well and I am certainly happy to help you find some solutions to your knee problems.Best, [Name]

Dear Mr. [Name],

Thank you for reaching out with your question. I understand that you are experiencing significant knee pain and are interested in a procedure involving cartilage culture and re-implantation.

This procedure, known as autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI), involves taking a small sample of cartilage from your knee, culturing the cells in a lab to grow more cartilage cells, and then re-implanting them into the damaged area of your knee. It is typically used for patients with cartilage damage that has not responded to other treatments.

To determine if this procedure would be suitable for you, we would need to conduct a thorough evaluation of your knee, including imaging studies and possibly an arthroscopic examination. I recommend scheduling an appointment with an orthopedic specialist who can assess your condition and discuss the potential benefits and risks of this procedure.

Please let us know if you would like us to arrange a referral to an orthopedic specialist or if you have any other questions.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name]
